questions.question: Differentiate between SRAM and DRAM with respect to their internal construction, refresh requirements, access speed, power consumption, cost per bit, and typical applications in computer systems.
keyPoints: SRAM uses 6 transistors per bit (flip-flop) vs DRAM uses 1 transistor + capacitor
DRAM requires periodic refresh (every 64ms) while SRAM is static and does not need refresh
SRAM is faster (2-10ns) but more expensive; DRAM is slower (50-100ns) but cheaper per bit
SRAM used for cache memory; DRAM used for main memory (RAM)

questions.question: Explain the concept of memory hierarchy in modern computer systems. Why is it impractical to use a single type of memory for all storage requirements?
keyPoints: Hierarchy: Registers → Cache (L1/L2/L3) → Main Memory (RAM) → Secondary Storage (HDD/SSD)
Trade-off between speed, cost, and capacity: faster memory is more expensive and has lower capacity
Principle of locality: temporal and spatial locality make caching effective
Without hierarchy, systems would be either prohibitively expensive or extremely slow

questions.question: Explain the role of CPU registers in instruction execution. Discuss the different types of registers (general purpose, special purpose, segment, flag/status registers) and their functions. Also explain how register-to-memory and memory-to-memory operations differ in terms of clock cycles and the importance of register file design in pipeline performance.
keyPoints: General purpose registers store operands and results; Special purpose include PC (program counter), MAR, MBR, IR; Segment registers for memory segmentation; Flag registers for status flags (Z, C, S, P)
Register-to-memory: 1-2 cycles (register read) + memory access (100+ cycles); Memory-to-memory requires loading to register first
Register file size and number of ports affect pipeline stalls and instruction-level parallelism
Register renaming helps resolve RAW, WAR, and WAW hazards in out-of-order execution

questions.question: Describe the evolution and characteristics of Read-Only Memory (ROM) types including Mask ROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, and Flash Memory. Explain how each achieves non-volatility and discuss their erase/write mechanisms. Additionally, compare RAM vs ROM in terms of volatility, read/write operations, cost, and applications, referencing bus architecture and memory mapping concepts.
keyPoints: Mask ROM: factory-programmed; PROM: one-time programmable via fuse burning; EPROM: UV-erasable (expensive, requires removal); EEPROM: electrically erasable byte-by-byte; Flash: block-wise erase, cheaper and faster than EEPROM
ROM non-volatility: data stored in floating gates (EEPROM/Flash) or permanent masks; no power required to maintain data
RAM: volatile, read/write both fast; ROM: non-volatile, primarily read-only (with exceptions like EEPROM/Flash)
Memory mapping: RAM mapped to contiguous addresses for user programs; ROM contains bootstrap loader at fixed addresses (e.g., 0xFFFF0 for x86); bus arbitration manages memory access conflicts
